transcript.text: Hi, Sierra.
Hi, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Good.
Nice to meet you.
You as well.
Where are you calling from?
I'm in Napa, California.
Oh, lovely.
How about yourself?
I am on the East Coast.
I'm in Rhode Island.
Nice.
Yeah.
So thank you for joining early in the morning.
Yeah, no problem at all.
Cool.
Well, I can tell you a little bit about this role and how I'm thinking about it.
I will be, like, the hiring manager.
manager.
So I'll be managing this person.
And then we can talk about you and then, you know, leave some time at the end for questions.
Pretty standard setup.
Yeah, but I, so I have been at GrowthX for seven months now, but we are about a year old.
So it's relatively legacy in the world of GrowthX.
We, I'm the first kind of like client ops hire.
And I essentially managed or like lead a lot of the day to day operations of the delivery org.
So, um, currently I'm managing like, um, everybody that's responsible for, uh, client facing work.
Um, but I think we're in the midst of planning out a reorg and this role, um, will help me actually like.
So get a little bit out of the weeds and also help action a lot of the operational efficiencies that we want to put in place.

You know, one of the things that we were doing and kind of put on pause until we hire this person is like try and stand up a more formal like project management tool that can help with capacity planning and a little bit more like C-site.
I saw that you have done some gain-site work, but, you know, we need somebody that will manage that in the end while I can keep an eye on like the larger business.
And, yeah, we're still relatively early.
think you're like the third person I'll have spoken to about this role, but also feeling super desperate about it.
Hopefully we'll be moving pretty quickly.
And I think, like, in terms of where this role goes, what it turns into, a lot of opportunity, obviously, right?
So, like,
Our delivery org is huge, has grown a ton, and will probably continue to change.
We have an internal platform that we're building that will probably launch.
So right now we're like services, a services business, but eventually we will also have a product and we'll be able to kind of have a self-serve product.
So opportunities to build on that side, lots of green space, lots to do.
So I think that's the most exciting thing about the role, but lots to do.
Yeah, totally get it.
Cool.
Well, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
Yeah.
So I first kind of started my professional career in customer support, and I enjoyed working with clients and just knowing that, you know, the work I was doing was having a direct impact.
However, support is very, you know, transactional relationship.
And so I wanted to move over to customer success and be on more of that relationship building side of things.
next time.
And see how I like that.
And so I moved to Brightwheel, and I was more on the customer success side.
I was helping clients get to their onboardings.
And then while I was there, I helped to develop and then manage their three-part webinar series, which was kind of the first step in the onboarding process for clients.
Basically, clients would attend this webinar, this webinar series, and throughout it, we would ask them questions.
And based on their responses, we would kind of send them down a high-touch or low-touch path.
And that would kind of help to decide what kind of onboarding path they would go down.

As we started, you know, having more customers, as well as making this, you know, the first step for every single customer in the onboarding process, we were seeing a lot of the operational side of things needing assistance.
And so we were able to kind of show the business that there was a need for...
Somebody to be just on the operations side of things to manage it.
And so me and a couple of other people kind of moved into that role.
And so it was a new role, didn't exist prior.
And so it was kind of cool to kind of chart that territory while we were in it.
And so I hope to just continue to make sure the webinars were running smoothly and run things on the back end.

And I found that this really married the two things that I loved, which was, you know, even though I wasn't still working with clients directly, I still knew that, you know, the impact that I was having on them and their onboarding process was great.
And then being able to do the operational side of things, which I found was really where my mind and kind of my process and workflow kind of just flowed better.
And so I moved to Resilience and I was their CS Ops manager.
I was the second hire after the director of customer success.
And so we didn't have CS team, we didn't have any processes, any workflows, nothing yet.
And so together, him and I really built the team and all of those things, the processes from the ground up.

And that was a really exciting time for me just to be able to build something that didn't exist, but also, you know, make sure that it was going to be scalable and make sure that, you know, the team was going to be able to do their work the best in that kind of way.
So, yeah, that's just kind of a quick snapshot of what I've been up to.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Well, can you give me an example of something that you built end to end and the process that went into that?
Yeah, definitely.
So one example that comes to mind is our customer feedback loop.
We didn't have a feedback loop when I joined and it was one of the first projects that I tackled.
So,
So first, just knowing that, you know, we needed to be able to get customer feedback back to the business, not only to our team, but to other teams like product and engineering, etc.

And so the way we did this was I helped or worked with, this was prior to me becoming a HubSpot admin, but I worked with our HubSpot team and we created a ticketing system within HubSpot, within the CRM.
So that way, we could capture this feedback.
And so CSMs would fill out specific fields, and then submit the feedback ticket based on feedback that they had gotten or received from a client, whether that's via email, or on calls live.
And so we needed a better way to get this information back to the business now that we were collecting it.
And so every single month, I would pull down the data, organize.
Based on the different types of fields that we had the CSMs filling out.
So sentiment, feedback type, things like that.
And then I would do a review of it with the product engineering and customer success teams.
Just to show them, you know, okay, these are the kinds of things that people are saying.
These are what they're wanting from us.
This is something that they're not wanting from us, et cetera.
Any kind of feedback, that's what we went over.
And this really had a huge impact on actually the product roadmap.
And it showed our product and engineering teams that, you know, maybe something they're planning on working on is actually not as important as we initially thought.
Or something that they had pushed till Q4, maybe we have to move it to Q1.
Because it's something that clients are really needing right now.
And so this just really allowed leadership as well as the rest of the business to just get better eyes on what's going on with clients.
And Thank
Have that ability to, you know, actually implement the kinds of things that clients were wanting.
Yeah, awesome.
Can you talk to me about implementing Gainsight and kind of what you did there?
Yeah, definitely.
That was a huge, huge take on for me.
Prior to that, I actually never worked even with Gainsight as an end user before.
And so when the director came to me and said, okay, we're going to get a CS tool and I need you to implement it.
I was like, okay, let's do it.
But kind of my process here was I learned from Gainsight.
They had trainings for me as the admin.
I learned from Gainsight, you know, how to set up things on the back end, what to do, et cetera.
And then I would take that back to my manager and we would discuss, you know, how's this going to work with our team and how we want it to look.
you.
Thank Thank
While I was figuring out the admin side of things, I sent all of the end user trainings to the team, because they also had never used Gainsight.
So it was very fresh and new for everybody.
And so while they were doing that, I was really working on building out everything on the back end.
And then every single week, I had an already standing meeting with the CSMs every week, just to kind of go over pain points, etc.
And I utilized that meeting to really go through a new feature that we were going to implement, or I was in the process of implementing.
Because I really found that, you know, implementing the system, and training the team, that was hard.
But the actual change management process of things for the team was arguably the hardest part.
And so being able to, you know, introduce to them slowly one new thing, and every week I would demo, demo it, I would give them the S.

So P that I had written for it on how we were going to approach it and what we were going to do, as well as giving them that supplemental gain site information as well, really helped to kind of, you know, lessen that blow of it being a whole new system that they had to learn and now utilize every single day.
And so I utilized those meetings to really just ensure that the team was on track with whatever I was implementing.
I also implemented more of a 85 to 15 rule.

And what I mean by that is, you know, I would get the feature that I was implementing to about 85% of the way complete, demo it for the team, then get all their feedback on it, hear what they thought, was this going to be, you know, was this going to be the way that they would like to see it?
What were their concerns?
What were their thoughts?
And this just really helped me to ensure that, you know, I was, I was taking in all of their.
And actually doing something with it.

then in turn, that helped the change management aspect of things because, you know, they were really trusting in the fact that I was committed to making this an actual tool for them and not for me, right?
Because even though I'm the one setting everything up, they're the ones that are going to be using it.
So at the end of the day, it really needed to work for them.
Um, and so, yeah, so weekly we would go through stuff and then the following week I would finish it and then we would kind of release it.

And so I think the combination of, you know, the online, you know, over Zoom demos, uh, the SOPs and videos that I had embedded within, um, the SOPs were super helpful for the team to get kind of caught up on what we were doing, um, as well as, you know, ensuring that I'm still implementing stuff week over week.
Yeah, um, perfect.
Perfect.
Uh, okay.
So, uh, uh, sorry.
Their efficiency or anything like that, we would focus on that first.
How would you measure that or kind of like who came up with those metrics?
Yeah, so mostly it was, you know, when we kind of first started, everything was manual.
And so that was a lot of the things that I was working on was turning all of our manual processes into automations.
And so I would do so, some automations would be built within Gainsight, and then some automations were built within our CRM HubSpot.
And so that was kind of the outlook that we had was, you know, let's take all of these manual processes.
Granted, some of them do have to be manual.
Some of them will have to be touched by a CSM.
But anything that we can automate, let's automate that.
And that was kind of the first way that we viewed it.
Yeah, okay.
I'm just taking notes.
Yeah, no problem.
Can you talk to me, as you were making these decisions, I'm sure you had some times when you had competing priorities to complete a deliverable.
So how did you handle resistance to process change and competing priorities across teams?
Yeah.
So the main way that I would do this was just to talk with the CSMs.
I like to have a really open relationship with them and just let them know that, you know, anything that I'm implementing is with their best interest.
And I think it's important to have that kind of open line of communication with them.
However, to your point, sometimes they are like, hey, this is more important or we want this more.
And so it's important, I think, for me to really take the conversations that I had with my manager or the director.
About, you know, why we were going to focus on X instead of Y, or why we were, you know, placing more of my time on one of the options versus the other, and really explain that to the team.

I think having that open line of communication is super important, and it was really beneficial in them not only understanding, like, hey, Y is still coming, right, but X just needs to get done first.
And I think sometimes, too, you know, I keep using this XY example, so sorry for that, but Y, you know, will be way easier to solve if I solve X first.

And so a lot of the things that we were doing with that kind of outlook and methodology, we were able to let the CSMs know and let them understand why we were doing things instead of just, these are the things we're doing, and this is the order that we're doing it.
But really, Y, know, you
The why behind it.
What's the project that you're most proud of that you implemented during your time?
Yeah.
I mean, honestly, I think one of the most important or like, you know, most ones that I'm proud of is definitely Gainsight just because I had never utilized it before.
However, since we already talked about that, I'll give you my second one, which would be our churn and renewal analysis.

Every single month, I would pull down data of, you know, a snapshot of where clients were at based on, you know, their product usage, their churn risk, et cetera, at that point in time of their renewal.

And then we would talk with the CSMs and, you know, the customer facing team about what's going on with the clients, how we can apply these learnings, whether they renewed or churned to upcoming renewals insurance.

And so it was really beneficial to really to just get the team together and look at their accounts and see if there are any similarities, what we can apply to different accounts and just have that kind of once a month where the team gets together and talks about their clients, you know, because I think it's easy for CSMs to kind of be siloed in their own book of business.
But really being able to take their own learnings and apply it to a different client, for example, really helped to improve our renewal rates.
Yeah, awesome.
You mentioned some automations.
Can you talk to me about like some examples of automations that you built?
Yeah.
So one specifically that comes to mind.
I worked very closely with our sales op person.
I think that, you know, just an overarching idea of mine that, you know, even though sales and customer success are two separate functions in a business.

They are very close together, and it was always important for me to not only be close and closely work with my sales kind of equivalent, but to ensure that any processes that I was thinking about would benefit sales and vice versa.
Some way the client had a nice, smooth customer experience and didn't feel that kind of shift between sales to CSS much.
And so something that I had worked and helped to build was an automated handoff.
So prior to this, we were actually doing handoffs via email.
And I'm sure, as you know, that is like very messy and can get very complicated and things can get lost very easily.
And so we knew that we needed a better way to do this.
And so we got together and we talked about, okay, what are the things like sales is filling out now?
What are the things CS needs to see when a client comes over to us?
And then while we were doing that, vice versa.
We also did a renewal handoff.
So what are the things that sales needs from CS when renewal time comes?
And so what we would do is we got all this information together.
And then at the point where the deal stage changes to closed one, we would have an automated handoff come to the CS ticketing queue.
And this would have all of the information that the CSM needs or wants going into the relationship.
And this often was information that, you know, was really difficult for the CSM to get prior.
Or they would have to, you know, send a slack because the email had the client on it.
So they couldn't, you know, reply all to the email, right?
So they'd have to send a separate message and it just got really messy.
And so really having all that information in one place at the time of handoff allowed for a much smoother transition internally and then led to, you know.
Externally, zero kind of friction between the two.
And so that's definitely something that really helped the team, both teams.
Awesome.
Yeah, cool.
Well, those are all the questions I had.
What questions do you have for me?
Yeah, I'm curious what you would say is something that this person can come in and do that you'd be like, wow, that was amazing.
Yeah, so that project management piece or implementing a project management platform for us would be huge.
We just put all of our directors into Linear for just kind of tracking effort and very similar to engineering.
But we need to tie, like, deals and opportunities with HubSpot and also, like, track kind of progress against contractual deliverables.
And, like, effort, things that are changing, and also account health scoring.
So we just implemented account health scoring.

But figuring out kind of how all those tie together and actually, like, getting into a tool, doing that, figuring out, like, the data piece and getting reporting for us that's a little bit more automated are top of mind for me.
Awesome.
And then would you say there are any kind of, like, challenges that the business is facing that this role could help with, aside from the items you mentioned previously?
Yeah, I mean, we are, we're re-orging right now because we, we need to think about our scalability and efficiency.
So we, this is mainly re-orging to set us up for success because we're taking on new clients every week.
It's a services business, so, like, two clients a week is a lot for...
we're...
We're like bringing on a services business.
Yeah.
And so we need to like keep thinking about operational efficiency, cost tracking, things like that.
So we're solving for that challenge, but it's something that we have to like really keep an eye on and that investors are like asking about a lot.
Right.
Totally.
Totally.
And then I guess in terms of, you know, this role and like kind of the team dynamic, how do you see this person kind of growing into the company and the role itself?
Yeah.
I mean, I need somebody who like is going to jump in, make decisions, take action like as soon as possible.
And that I think means that this person can go in whatever direction where they see like space that they're excited about.
So, yeah, like I, I don't want to prescribe a career pathing on this because.
I think there are so many options, depending on what we end up building.
Right.
Yeah.
No, that's great to hear.
I love that ambiguity about it.
That's what's fun about working at startups.

I've really only ever worked at startups, and so the environment, I feel like, is really fun and exciting because there's always stuff going on and places where you can show your strengths and try something new.
And, yeah, I love that.
Love that for sure.
And then my last question, what about GrowthX do you love?
Like, why do you love working here?
I mean, I think that the problem, our business model is so interesting because it is, like, services-based, but we're building a product platform.
So I worked at, like, a SaaS startup before this, which was, you know, like, you're just trying to get, like, as many clients as possible.
Yeah.
Before you run out of money, it's like running up a gravel hill.
And I feel like this is, we have all the, we have like a really strong financial base, which is awesome.
Like we can feel confident that we have the budget and space to build the product.
And we're using the product internally.
And so we're the ones that are making it better.
So it's like confidence building.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's awesome.
Yeah.
Those are the only questions I have, but that all sounds really great.
And I'm really excited about the role.
Awesome.
Well, Sierra, was great chatting with you.
And yeah, have a good rest of your day.
Thanks, Sandy.
Bye.
